title: What an American thinks about Argentina

transcript.text: Okay, what an American thinks about Argentina.
Well, I was in Argentina for a bad time of their time.
This was back in like 2000.
And I worked there for two months teaching English and didn't get paid a penny.
Because they kept telling me they'd pay me later and pay me later.
And then I traveled for two months through Brazil and stuff.
And I came back and they still couldn't pay me, couldn't pay me after three weeks.
Then they gave me a check.
And the check was from a bank that went under because the two brothers who owned it,
One of them was in jail and the other one disappeared because he stole like $300 million or something.
And I was there when they devalued.
Their peso was pegged to the dollar, one peso to one dollar.
And then when I left, it was three and a half pesos to a dollar.
But I heard before they devalued, all their politicians and rich people and stuff were
Carting cash out of the country like in semi-trucks.
That's just what some dude in Uruguay told me.
They had protests where people were clanging on the pots and pans in the streets
And setting bonfires in the middle of the streets.
But the Argentinians themselves, they're really well-dressed.
Like, you know, when you look at them walking through town, they look almost like a European country,
You know, first world country.
They're dressed in leather a lot.
They make a lot of leather.
I think that's what they export, the cows.
And they're white.
They're pretty white looking, you know, just like you or like me.
And their English is pretty good.
Better than anybody else in South America.
But they were really grumpy while I was there.
One time I was on a payphone and I was just trying to get people passing by to show me how to put the money in
Because it was confusing me.
It was a weird kind of payphone.
And I literally couldn't get anybody to show me because I, and they were rude too.
They were like, ah.
And I think it was because they thought I was panhandling.
Maybe because I didn't see that many panhandlers there, but, um, yeah, they were really stressed out over there.
One lady asked me for money and I gave her some and then she got pissed.
She said it was for a charity.
Then she got all pissed off at me.
She's like, what am I going to do with this?
This is nothing.
You have it back.
I got like angry with me.
But I was looking at her a lot and, um, she, uh, she didn't ask anybody else for money.
She was just, like, sitting down for, like, an hour.
The, um, the subways are really old.
One of the subway cars I was riding on, I think it was from, like, World War I.
Like, every time the track changed, shifted, the whole thing, the whole subway car, like, shifted.
Like, one of these old wooden-built houses that are, like, just about to fall over.
The whole thing was, like, made out of wood.
And the tunnels walking into the subway were, like, really little.
Like, they were dug by some dude with a shovel.
And, uh, yeah, and there were a lot of old buildings.
Yeah, but a lot of cracks and stuff on the sidewalk.
They definitely were having money problems.
One fucked up thing about Argentina, or Buenos Aires, is, um, is that, uh, it's right next to the Plata River.
But you can't see the other side of the river, because it's at the opening of it to the ocean.
And you can't, but you can't see it.
You might as well be in some town, a normal town.
But, yeah, they built its back to the, to the river.
It's stupid.
All the, like, industry is there.
You can't, there's only one way to access it.
And you have to go down this one narrow trail and way out.
And then you get, like, a little bit of this rocky area.
But, uh, yeah.
But the people I did the English for, they were, yeah, I don't, that wasn't cool how they didn't pay me.
Um, and then when they paid me, they said I had to, like, get legal and go to this thing and wait in line.
And then I go there, and it wasn't.
So they didn't know what they were doing, or they were just trying to, like, delay me or something.
What they usually do with teachers, I heard from other teachers, is that they don't pay, they never pay anybody on time.
It's always a month late, so they can get more money on interest on the, on the, uh, from the bank.
When you do their classes, you have to be in long pants, though.
They're really formal.
I showed up in shorts one day, and, like, the person complained and said I needed to show up in pants.
But the teachers, I don't know if it was the person I did the class with, but the students I did, they were really good at English.
It was just conversation.
But, uh, the city's really big, and it's really, um, the, the, the nice part of town is nice.
The houses are nice, but they're all gated.
They're like the big, you know, big, um, fences in front of the house.
It's, yeah.
The southern part of this country is really windy.
There's this one town called Montezillo or something.
It was really, really windy.
Like, insanely windy.
Outside of it.
Once you go in it, and it's not as windy from the, being sheltered by the big buildings.
It was like, it was like Star Wars.
You know?
Where if you go outside of the city, it's like, watch out!
You're in the wilderness now!
Because it was so windy.
And I took a bus into this, um, to see this, um, glacier, Moreno.
And I was the only guy in the whole bus.
It was expensive, too.
It was really expensive there.
Like, as expensive as America when I was there.
And I did the tour, and this, uh, Argentinian dude offered to pay for my special, like, little boat trip next to the, um, glacier.
I didn't accept, uh, but yeah.
We were pretty mellow, though.
I got really drunk and went into this room full of these dudes, and I was all being annoying.
Like, where can I buy the weed?
And they were just like, but, um, yeah.
A lot of them were grumpy looking on the streets in Buenos Aires.
There was not a good time for them.
Like, yelling, too.
Like, you ask them, and they just, like, yell.
They get aggressive.
The Argentinians are more aggressive than, than, like, anybody else in South America.
Including the, uh, Chileans.
Chileans aren't like that.
And they like to talk.
Argentinians, they'll talk forever about, like, about nothing.
About really trivial stuff.
I like people who talk, but only if they, like, know stuff.
They talk about, like, their relatives and stuff.
Their relatives' dogs and stuff.
They just like to talk.
They'll talk forever.
Um, like, ones you meet, just people you meet on the street, on the bus and stuff.
The tango is interesting.
They do the tango dance.
It's, like, their own, their very own art form.
It's kind of like an old, it's like an olden time.
It's, it's like going back in time.
Um, I guess one of the Argentinian guys told me that in world, in the 50s, it was, like,
The sixth richest country in the world.
So it was totally first world country.
And then it collapsed after that.
But it kind of, I had that feeling.
I had that kind of feeling like it was nostalgic.
They were nostalgic for the past.
Just, they dressed kind of, like, from the past.
And, you know, the men stand really upright like this.
And, you know, they do the tango with that old music.
And the buildings are really old and big.
And kind of architecture similar to Paris.
Big buildings.
Um, yeah.
It's actually kind of a, has a cool feel to it.
Okay, so that's what I think about Argentina.
Amen.
